angle: Envelope-fidelity enforcement in the /memory:search command: making requestQuality/citationPolicy deterministic and model-independent at render time.
Envelope-fidelity enforcement in the /memory:search command: making requestQuality/citationPolicy deterministic and model-independent at render time.
title: Promote requestQuality/citationPolicy from "optional" to a mandatory core slot whenever the tool response carries them
problem: The verdict the benchmark cares about is computed deterministically tool-side and shipped as structured JSON, yet the render contract declares it optional, so a model that drops it is fully contract-compliant. In the tool: formatters/search-results.ts:1087 computes requestQualityData via assessRequestQuality and :1161 computes citationPolicy via deriveCitationPolicy, and both are spread into the MCP success response data at :1173 (`...requestQualityData`) and :1176 (`citationPolicy`). So on every non-empty result the model RECEIVES both fields in the tool result — it never computes them. But the render contract treats them as droppable: search.md:76 says "Their absence is valid" and search_presentation.txt:103 + :116 repeat "requestQuality and citationPolicy are the only sanctioned extras" / "Absence is valid." The benchmark confirms this is exactly where fidelity is lost: extract-metrics.mjs:53-54 scrapes the verdict from the model's RENDERED text and :62 scores fidelity as `rq != null`; benchmark-results.md:79 reports all five cross-model disagreements are a null (omitted) field, and implementation-summary.md:59 states "the only cross-model variance is in envelope fidelity, whether a model renders the full contract." The field is present and correct in the tool JSON every time; the variance is purely whether weak models copy it into prose.
proposal: Edit the render contract so requestQuality/citationPolicy become MANDATORY (a sixth and seventh core slot) on any non-empty result WHENEVER they are present in the tool-response data, instead of "sanctioned optional extras." Concretely: in search.md §3 change the "Named optional fields" clause (:76) and the §3 "Core-slot mandate" (:68) to add a conditional-required rule — "if the tool response data contains requestQuality or citationPolicy, both MUST be rendered as named trailing fields; omission is a contract violation, not a valid absence." Mirror the same change in search_presentation.txt §2 (:102-116), replacing "Absence is valid" with "Absence is valid ONLY when the tool response omits the field; if the tool returned it, render it." Keep the existing exact field-name placement (between the scored block and the STATUS footer) unchanged. This is the lowest-risk fix because the data is already there — it only closes the policy loophole that lets the model treat a present field as optional.
benefit: Removes the sole observed source of cross-model envelope variance. Under the new contract a model that drops requestQuality is non-compliant rather than acceptable, the necessary precondition for any downstream automated check (Proposal 2/3) to flag it. Expected to lift the weak drivers (gpt-5.5 medium at 5.6/7, Kimi at 6.5/7 per benchmark-results.md:24-27) toward the DeepSeek 6.9/7 ceiling, since the two missing slots are exactly requestQuality/citationPolicy.
risk: Low. The change is prose-only in two contract files and asserts something the tool already guarantees (the field is present). The one edge to preserve: when the tool legitimately omits the field (the no-flag/disabled path — formatters/search-results.ts:832 only attaches requestQualityData when isResultConfidenceEnabled()), the contract must still allow absence, so the rule must be conditioned on presence-in-data, not unconditional. Wording it as "if present in the tool response" avoids forcing the model to fabricate a verdict the tool never sent.

angle: Envelope-fidelity enforcement in the /memory:search command: making requestQuality/citationPolicy deterministic and model-independent at render time.
title: Add a deterministic post-render envelope-fidelity check that replays the tool verdict against the rendered block
problem: There is no deterministic enforcement that the rendered block matches the tool-computed verdict — enforcement is entirely soft prose the model self-administers. search.md:78 is a "Self-check" instruction ("verify the emitted block includes the MEMORY:SEARCH header, all five core slots, and the STATUS footer") and search_presentation.txt:162 is a "Pre-render gate" that only scans for forbidden vocabulary. Neither checks that requestQuality/citationPolicy survived into the output, and both run inside the model. The benchmark had to reconstruct fidelity externally with regex (extract-metrics.mjs:60-63) precisely because the command ships no such check. This is the structural reason a weak model silently produces a low-fidelity block: nothing compares what the tool returned to what the model rendered.
proposal: Introduce a small deterministic verifier the command runs after the model drafts the block but before final emit, analogous to the existing §0 bash arg-resolver in search.md:17. Given (a) the tool-response JSON and (b) the candidate rendered text, it asserts: the rendered requestQuality value equals data.requestQuality.label, the rendered citationPolicy equals data.citationPolicy, both are present iff the tool returned them, and cite-iff-good holds (citationPolicy == 'cite_results' exactly when requestQuality == 'good' — the exact rule deriveCitationPolicy enforces at formatters/search-results.ts:368-371). The verifier's own logic can be lifted verbatim from extract-metrics.mjs:53-66, which already implements the regex extraction and the citeExpected/citeCorrect comparison. Wire it as a command-side script reference (the command already reads assets and runs a bash preamble), keeping it model-independent.
benefit: Turns envelope fidelity from a model-dependent property into a deterministic gate the weakest model still passes, because the check, not the model, is the authority. It catches both omissions (the 5 null cases in benchmark-results.md:79) and any future drift where a model paraphrases the verdict instead of copying it. It also gives the contract a machine-checkable definition of 'fidelity' that today only exists in the benchmark's throwaway parser.
risk: Medium. A post-render re-emit loop adds a round-trip and must be bounded (cap at one retry) so a pathological model cannot loop. The verifier must read the verdict from the structured tool JSON, not re-scrape the model's prose, or it inherits the same fragility it is meant to remove. It must also no-op cleanly on the empty-result path (formatters/search-results.ts:846 createMCPEmptyResponse still sets citationPolicy but RESULTS=0 uses a different template, search_presentation.txt:136).

angle: Envelope-fidelity enforcement in the /memory:search command: making requestQuality/citationPolicy deterministic and model-independent at render time.
title: Make the renderer non-discretionary by having the tool emit a ready-to-paste envelope line, removing the model's transcription step
problem: Even with a mandatory-slot rule (Proposal 1) and a post-check (Proposal 2), the model still has to TRANSCRIBE two structured fields into two specific prose lines in a specific position (search_presentation.txt:104-114 dictates exact placement between the scored block and the STATUS footer). Transcription is the step weak models fail — implementation-summary.md:63 notes Kimi drops the field when 'the long exploration costs it the envelope field entirely,' i.e. the field is lost during the model's own rendering, not during retrieval. The tool already holds the canonical values (formatters/search-results.ts:1173,1176) but exposes them only as nested JSON the model must reshape into the contract's exact two-line trailing format.
proposal: Have the formatter additionally emit a pre-rendered, contract-exact envelope fragment in the tool response — e.g. data.renderedEnvelope = "requestQuality good\ncitationPolicy cite_results" built directly from requestQualityData.requestQuality.label and the citationPolicy string at the point they are computed (formatters/search-results.ts:1160-1161). The command contract then instructs the model to paste data.renderedEnvelope verbatim into the trailing-field slot rather than reconstruct it from the nested confidence object. This collapses the failure-prone transcription into a copy of a single string. It is additive (a new data field), so it does not disturb existing consumers reading data.requestQuality / data.citationPolicy.
benefit: Eliminates the transcription degree-of-freedom that causes the omissions, making the rendered envelope as model-robust as the citationPolicy obedience already is (benchmark cite-correct rate 1.0 across all models per benchmark-results.md:29 — models reliably OBEY the policy; they only fail to RENDER it). Pairs naturally with Proposal 2, whose check then reduces to a string-equality between data.renderedEnvelope and the emitted lines.
risk: Medium. Adds a presentation concern (exact contract formatting) into the tool formatter, which currently emits only structured data and leaves rendering to the command — a separation-of-concerns regression that must be weighed. If the contract's trailing-field format ever changes, two places (the asset and the formatter string) must move together or drift. Lower priority than 1/2 for that reason; it is the strongest fix but the most invasive to the tool/command boundary.

angle: Envelope-fidelity enforcement in the /memory:search command: making requestQuality/citationPolicy deterministic and model-independent at render time.
title: OUT-OF-ANGLE NOTE: the kubernetes false-relevance is score calibration, not envelope fidelity
problem: The brief flags the kubernetes-at-0.78-good false positive, but reading the code confirms this is NOT fixable in the command/render layer this angle owns — it is upstream score calibration. assessRequestQuality (confidence-scoring.ts:471-477) returns 'good' whenever topScore >= TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD (0.8) OR topScore >= HIGH_THRESHOLD (0.7) with margin/ratio satisfied; the 0.78 kubernetes hit clears the 0.7+margin path deterministically. benchmark-results.md:81 and implementation-summary.md:67 both attribute it to 'the absolute-relevance calibration over-scoring an off-corpus term' that 'the models faithfully reported.' The envelope did its job perfectly here: it deterministically reported good because the upstream score said 0.78. No template-enforcement, post-render check, or tool-emitted-verdict change in the command would alter this — the verdict is correct given the input; the INPUT (the absolute-relevance score) is the soft spot.
proposal: Explicitly scope this out of the envelope-fidelity angle and route it to the calibration angle. The fix space lives in resolveAbsoluteRelevance / the absolute-relevance calibration model (confidence-scoring.ts:170-174, 381) and the 0.8/0.7/0.4 thresholds — e.g. an off-corpus / out-of-distribution guard that lowers the score before it reaches the gate, or a margin-to-corpus-centroid penalty. None of that belongs in search.md or search_presentation.txt.
benefit: Prevents wasted effort trying to patch a calibration miss in the render contract, and keeps the envelope-fidelity proposals (1-3) clean and correctly scoped. Documents that the envelope is functioning as designed on this case.
risk: Low (documentation/scoping only). The risk is the opposite — that a reviewer conflates the two soft spots and tries to harden the render contract against a spurious score, which cannot work because the contract is downstream of the score and is behaving correctly.
